data.title: Trump Tariffs To Drive Up Car Prices And Dealer Profit Margins
data.description:
Originally published on April 2, 2025

President Trump’s new tariffs on cars will raise prices and also reduce hard information about car deals. Even astute car dealers may be confused, and consumers have little chance of understanding what is really happening when they buy a new car - except prices are higher. In many cases, though, car dealers will enjoy higher profit margins.

The economics of tariffs is well-understood by economists - until the specific details of a transaction are considered. The deeper one goes into the arcane world of car manufacturers’ terms of sale, the more complicated pricing becomes. But we can get a solid understanding that reaches two conclusions: car buyers will pay more, and dealer profit margins for U.S.-made automobiles will increase.

The Basic Economics Of Tariffs

Take a simple situation, in which American-made products compete against comparable imported goods. In the United States, about 60% of new cars are assembled in the U.S. and the rest imported. A tariff on imports has several effects. First, some foreign manufacturers would accept lower pre-tariff prices to prevent too steep a sales decline. They would likely continue to cover their variable costs, such as the cost of materials and direct labor required to make a car. They would not meet their previous profit margin, and possibly not even their fixed costs. Generally, we expect most but not all of the tariff to be passed through to consumers. The specific amount passed through depends on consumers’ demand elasticity as well as the cost structure of the manufacturers.

The second effect would be that prices facing American buyers for imported cars would rise. This price increase would equal that portion of the tariff that foreign manufacturers do not absorb, probably well over half.

The third impact will be a price hike for American-made cars. (And note that the nameplate - Ford or Fiat - does not tell us where the car was actually assembled.) The rise in domestic car prices results from what economists call “The Law of One Price,” which states that two similar products sold in the same market will trade at the same price. In simple terms, the sellers of domestic cars will raise their prices because they can. If they did not, nobody would buy the imported cars; everyone would buy the domestic cars. This is one reason that foreign manufacturers will cut their prices somewhat. Domestic prices will rise because manufacturers will not be able to serve all buyers at their previous, pre-tariff prices. The primary way to ration the excess demand is to raise prices. (Below, we consider President Trump’s admonition to car companies not to raise their prices.)

With higher prices for new domestic and imported cars, used car prices will rise. Some car buyers are on the fence between a new car and a late-model used car. Higher prices on new cars will tip them toward used. The used car price increases will be highest for the most recent used cars. Very old used cars will see a much smaller price increase.

Economists have studied tariffs for over 200 years, and this description reflects a very strong consensus. But we can add further information for a more accurate - and complicated - conclusion.

Tariffs On Materials Used In Domestic Production

The complete tariff policy of the Trump administration will likely change after this writing, but here we consider tariffs on imported materials used by domestic manufacturers. In some cases, car makers use imported steel and aluminum. They may also import whole engines, transmissions and other components.

The foreign companies that sell the materials may eat some of the tariffs themselves. This will be more common for complicated manufactured products, such as engines, and less common for commodities, such as steel. Commodity producers can route their materials to countries without tariffs, or with lower tariffs. A company that makes a particular type of engine may not have good options, in the short run, to sell engines somewhere else. That business would be more likely to pay some of the tariff themselves, especially in the short run.

With higher costs of production, domestic manufacturers would pass some or all of their tariff expense on to consumers. With a tariff on imported cars at the same time, a higher percentage of the materials tariff would be passed on to buyers.

The tariff on input materials further adds to the increase in domestic car prices that the car-specific tariff would trigger. The price increase for domestic cars would enable foreign producers to pass on to consumers more of their tariffs.

Domestic Car Prices Cannot Be Kept From Increasing

President Trump told car manufacturers not to raise their prices after he imposes tariffs. Although the president may not have authority to enforce that request, he certainly can make life very unpleasant for companies that ignore him. But here is where complications arise.

When a car dealer buys a vehicle from the manufacturer, the deal is very complicated. We begin with an invoice price, which sounds simple. This is a price that dealers may show to customers to emphasize the low profit margins.

However, “dealer holdback” is the common practice whereby a discount of two to three percent is provided to the dealer some months after the transaction. The sales person at the car lot can show an invoice price but conceal this kickback. The holdback percentage varies with the make and model of the vehicle, and not all manufacturers offer it.

Some manufacturers offer floor plan assistance. This is a short-term loan to enable the dealer to buy cars that will be available to consumers for immediate purchase. The manufacturer can help the dealer by offering a below-market interest rate.

“Factory to dealer incentives” give further discounts to dealers for specific makes and models. They come and go as the manufacturer needs to move certain products.

Most manufacturers run cooperative advertising programs. They encourage dealers to advertise the brand, and they may want dealer support for regional or national advertising. This is another movement of money between manufacturers and dealers.

Volume bonuses are paid to dealers by manufacturers when they want to move a particular make or model car. “Dealer cash” is another program that manufacturers use to incent dealers to sell particular models.

Destination charges are typically shrugged off by consumers as a necessary expense, representing transportation charges as the vehicle moves from factory to dealer. But they may exceed actual costs, another way to add to manufacturer revenue without increasing invoice prices.

Speaking to car dealers recently, I said, “I’m just a simple boy with a Ph.D. in economics. Your deals with manufacturers are too complicated for me.”

The bottom line is that dealers can conform to the president’s request for them not to raise prices, while raising prices in a myriad of obscure ways.

Dealer Profit Margins Will Rise On Domestic Cars

Car dealers will face significant changes, some positive and some negative. Dealer margins on domestic products will certainly increase. Prices will rise so that the quantity demanded matches the supply. Manufacturers will capture some of the higher price, but pressure from the White House will probably prevent them from pushing prices all the way up to the market-clearing level. That will leave dealers to allocate the limited supply. They will forget about all of the discounts that have been offered to buyers in the past. They won’t discount from the manufacturer’s suggested retail price and will even add additional margin. They may bundle a basic car with dealer-supplied add-ons, with no bare-bones alternative available.

Foreign-made cars brought in before tariffs will be priced high, generating high dealer profits. But some dealers will have committed to buying cars only to learn that the vehicles have tariffs slapped on them, sometimes while the ship is anchored waiting to unload. Dealers will probably take a loss on those cars. And if they have to keep some tariffed cars in stock, because that is their brand, they will have to accept low profit margins.

Dealers with plenty of late-model used cars in stock will enjoy the tariffs, which will enable them to boost their prices.

Getting the right mix of cars on the lot will be critical for car dealers’ success.

Tariffs On Cars: Conclusion

President Trump’s tariffs on imported cars and light trucks, combined with general tariffs that include materials used to make cars, will raise prices that buyers face, whether they are buying foreign or domestic cars, or even used cars.

Dealer profit margins will increase on domestically-produced cars, but drop on imports. Dealers with late-model used cars on their lots will pocket extra cash.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Simulations Plus: I See Promising Growth, But I'm Staying Neutral
data.description:
Thesis

Simulations Plus, Inc. (NASDAQ:SLP), which is a specialized provider of biosimulation and predictive modeling software, has, over time, established a rather distinctive position at the point where pharmaceutical research and development meets advanced computational science. It is best known for its GastroPlus and ADMET Predictor platforms, which equip drug developers with AI-enhanced tools that allow them to simulate how drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and excreted. In doing so, the company, at least to a significant extent, helps to streamline the design of clinical trials and, at the same time, to optimize therapeutic strategies.

I initiated coverage on SLP last summer with a Hold rating, largely because, although I was encouraged by the company’s solid performance in the third quarter, not least the 9% revenue growth, which was driven by its flagship software products, I did have a few reservations about how sustainable its profitability might actually be. The falling gross margins, along with rising operational costs and a dwindling backlog of services projects, seemed to point to possible pressure on future earnings.

With the Q2 2025 earnings just out yesterday, I took the opportunity to go through the numbers, and it seems to me that, while its differentiated AI-driven platforms and solid balance sheet do offer clear advantages in what is an increasingly competitive biosimulation landscape, the real key to creating lasting shareholder value lies rather squarely with management. Specifically, it depends on their ability to turn the backlog into profitable revenue, to get margins back on track, and to show, with some consistency, that the expanded range of offerings can actually win market share, even in what remains a fairly cautious and capital-constrained environment.

Underappreciated Growth Momentum

Initially, as I went over the new data, I came away with a growing conviction that beneath the noise of transient margin compression, there is a quietly compounding engine in motion, and it’s accelerating faster than the market appears to appreciate.

The headline numbers were clean: $22.43 million in revenue, up 22.55% year-over-year, comfortably ahead of expectations, and EPS of $0.15, a three-cent beat.

Importantly, adjusted EPS held steady at $0.31, flat versus the prior year but significant in context given the sector-wide margin pressures and customer spending hesitancy gripping the broader biotech landscape.

What really caught my eye wasn’t just the topline growth on its own, but the makeup of that growth. Software, which is very much the backbone of SLP’s high-margin model, grew by 16% year over year, and by 8% organically, which is a pretty respectable figure, especially when you take into account the erratic spending patterns of biotech and CRO clients that have kept even the leading businesses in the sector on edge.

This highlights an important nuance: SLP’s platforms, particularly GastroPlus and ADMET Predictor, are increasingly non-discretionary assets for their users. Even in cautious spending environments, customers continue renewing and expanding licenses, underlining SLP’s deeply embedded role in pharma R&D workflows.

QSP Software Surge

Of particular note was the performance of their QSP software, which saw an 89% increase, largely driven by a marquee license for atopic dermatitis modeling. CEO O’Connor was candid here, explaining that these deals are lumpy due to their high-value and specialized nature, but emphasized the strategic importance in that the QSP software disease model licensing is a very good growth area for them and that their smaller licenses will be more consistent on a quarterly basis, and on a year-over-year basis, they should see good growth from the large disease models.

This lines up perfectly with the secular trend toward more integrated, systems pharmacology approaches in drug development, as companies prioritize models that can simulate disease progression and optimize trial design. This is precisely where SLP’s AI/ML integrations shine. Their platforms, particularly GastroPlus, use embedded machine learning algorithms to better forecast human responses to experimental drugs, a clear differentiator versus CROs or internal tools at pharmas struggling to replicate this depth without heavy upfront investment.

Another bright spot is the $5 million multi-drug project in the Medical Communications (MC) unit, which will ramp in the second half of the year and continue into 2026. This contract materially strengthens the visibility of services revenue, which has otherwise been hampered by client delays in project starts. That services backlog now stands at $20.4 million, up 18% sequentially, providing not just revenue visibility but also a springboard for second-half acceleration.

Pipeline Expansion Potential

The pipeline commentary on the conference call was also pointedly bullish.

Management described the opportunity set as quite "active," particularly highlighting cross-selling with their newly integrated Pro-ficiency and MC units. These integrations are beginning to unlock new layers of customer engagement, a strategic payoff from recent M&A that, if successfully executed, could meaningfully expand wallet share within existing accounts. In fact, they’re already seeing results: average software revenue per customer climbed to $124,000, up from $113,000 a year prior.

Confident Full-Year Outlook

What might also be somewhat reassuring is management’s decision to reaffirm the full-year guidance, which, in the current tariff-uncertainty climate, feels like a welcome, confident move, especially given that many of their peers are busy trimming forecasts under the weight of all the prevailing macro pressures. They’re still expecting revenue to come in between $90 million and $93 million, which works out to growth of 28% to 33%, along with adjusted EBITDA margins of 31% to 33%, and adjusted EPS somewhere between $1.07 and $1.20. This isn’t, by the look of it, just a placeholder; rather, it reflects a genuine belief that project initiations will pick up in the second half, and that software renewals will go on providing healthy recurring revenue streams.

Finally, the balance sheet, a quiet but critical strength here, with $21.4 million in cash and short-term investments and zero debt, provides both defensive ballast and offensive flexibility. As O’Connor noted, they remain well-positioned to "step up if the market picks up and the gates open in terms of spending." The takeaway here is that in this environment, where plenty of smaller peers are scrambling around with capital constraints, this actually counts as a pretty meaningful competitive advantage.

Valuation and Growth Potential

The company’s current P/E ratio of 27.89x already reflects, at least in part, the market’s ongoing recalibration from its earlier peak multiple of 48.6x back in 2024, moving more or less in line with the broader pullback in healthcare technology valuations. That said, this compression isn’t necessarily something to be alarmed about. If anything, it suggests that, while investors are understandably very cautious for the time being, they still see the earnings potential that SLP is likely to deliver once its operational improvements begin to take hold.

Forward estimates reinforce this inflection point.

Consensus sees the P/E ratio trending lower to 37.5x in 2026 and 29.22x by 2027, which might suggest multiple contraction, but it's important to view this in context with projected earnings growth.

EPS growth expectations accelerate sharply from a muted 0.51% in August 2025 to 28.93% in August 2026, and 28.35% in 2027. If these numbers materialize, investors today are essentially locking in high-teens to high-twenties earnings growth at a mid-20s earnings multiple three years out, not a bad trade-off in a market increasingly skeptical of premium-priced growth stocks.

So, as much as I’m encouraged by SLP’s resilience on the top line, it would be hard to overlook the growing cracks beneath the surface, and, to be fair, they do seem to be widening in ways that deserve some careful attention. For all the optimism about revenue beats and the promise of the pipeline, the reality is that SLP’s margin erosion, weakening cash flow, and uneven operational pacing together present a narrative that feels, if anything, more fragile in terms of near-term fundamentals than the headline growth numbers might lead us to believe.

Let’s start where investors instinctively look first: margin profile. The extent of the contraction this quarter is not something you should dismiss as noise. Total gross margin plunged to 59% from 72%, a dramatic decline for what is, in essence, a premium-priced software and services platform. Software gross margins fell to 81% from 88%, while services margin all but collapsed to 25% from 44%. The drivers here are painfully clear. There was a $4.2 million increase in cost of revenues, with $0.8 million of that tied to amortization from the Pro-ficiency acquisition, and an additional $0.4 million in amortization of capitalized software costs. That’s before we even factor in the $3 million spike in services costs, largely from MC expansion and reclassification of expenses.

This is not just typical post-acquisition integration turbulence. It signals a deeper operational strain. When I see services margin erode like this, especially given that management had flagged the strength of the services backlog, I have to question the timing and efficiency of project execution. In fact, management conceded on the call that clients are dragging their feet, that is, clients are contracting for work effort that is scheduled to be initiated later in the year, O’Connor admitted. This pacing issue is now visibly impairing profitability, despite the seemingly healthy growth in bookings.

It bleeds further into net income.

At $3.1 million, down from $4 million, net income margins contracted to 14%, a sharp reversal from the prior year’s 22%. Diluted EPS slid to $0.15 from $0.20, and adjusted EBITDA of $6.6 million versus $7.1 million last year dragged the EBITDA margin down from 39% to 29%. These are material declines, not rounding errors. What’s more telling is that this degradation is happening even as management ramps up spending aggressively: sales and marketing spend ballooned to 17% of revenue from 11%, and R&D swelled to 10% of revenue, up from 7%, as SLP tries to build out its newer business units and defend its competitive position.

Cash Flow Pressures

And then there’s cash flow, my biggest concern in the short term.

Levered free cash flow collapsed 37.3% year over year, and forward free cash flow per share growth is projected at a worrying -3.91%. Operating cash flow is down 31.35%, which management tried to position as timing-related, citing service delays and project starts skewing toward the back half of fiscal 2025. But this delay-driven cash flow compression is not comforting in the current macro environment, especially for a company that relies heavily on upfront client payments for multi-year software and services contracts.

Macro headwinds, it has to be said, only add to these concerns. Biotech funding remains tight, and while Big Pharma has managed to keep up its R&D spending, SLP’s small to mid-cap biotech clients are, by contrast, under considerable capital strain. Add to that the broader uncertainties, everything from tariff risks to manufacturing disruptions, and you end up with a situation where customers may well continue to delay their spending, stretching out sales cycles at exactly the time when SLP is trying to manage the higher operating costs that have come with its recent acquisitions.

Now, the balance sheet I mentioned earlier remains a safety net. This allows management to continue investing aggressively in R&D and sales enablement. But while the balance sheet buys time, it doesn’t buy a free pass. If backlog conversion slips further or if margins fail to stabilize by the second half of fiscal 2025, SLP’s premium valuation, already trimmed down to that of 27.89x P/E, with consensus expecting a further compression by 2027, could come under sharper pressure.

Final Rating: Hold

I’m sticking with my Hold rating for SLP. The strength in their software growth and the promising bits in the pipeline are encouraging enough, but then I look at the persistent margin erosion, the cash flow pressures, and the sluggish service execution, and my enthusiasm starts to fade. What I’d really like, before upgrading to a "Buy", is some clearer proof that they can actually sort out their operations and turn that backlog into something useful. For the time being, the risk and reward seem to be cancelling each other out nicely, and I’d much rather wait to see if management can get profitability back on its feet and keep growth from wandering off.

data.title: Why A Barbell Income Portfolio Makes Sense Today
data.description:
A barbell portfolio is one of the many ways to manage an investment allocation. In this article, we take a look at this allocation type in the context of income investing. In our view, barbell portfolios have a lot to recommend them. Although they don't typically sport the highest yields, they carry a number of benefits that can allow them to outperform over the longer term. Below we explore their features and benefits, how they can apply to income investing and what types of securities can work in their construction.

What Is A Barbell Portfolio?

There are many different types of barbell portfolios. Conceptually, the idea of a barbell portfolio is that rather than holding everything along the asset spectrum, an investor instead picks two different (i.e., perhaps polar opposite, hence the barbell name) types of assets in the portfolio. The idea is that because the two assets will tend to behave differently in various market scenarios, their combination provides both participation in the market as well as diversification.

In the context of income investing, "quality" tends to be the key differentiator, with a common barbell composed of higher quality / lower-yielding and lower-quality / higher-yielding assets. However, in our view, quality is not expansive enough a criterion. It can also mislead investors into thinking that, say, a portfolio composed of CLO Equity CEFs and Muni CEFs is adequate because it spans the gamut of credit quality.

In our view, rather than quality, investors should think about "risk" as the key differentiator between the two barbell assets. "Quality" tends to stand in for sector (i.e., junk bonds are lower-quality; investment-grade bonds are higher-quality), and therefore, it ignores many other factors that make a barbell portfolio work.

Specifically, we would focus on four different risk factors to vary across the income barbell – sector, leverage, duration and discounts. Both of these factors have an impact on both yield and risk that a given asset produces.

Taking sector first, higher-risk sectors include loans and high-yield corporate bonds, while lower-risk sectors include munis and preferreds. By sector risk we primarily mean market volatility and likelihood of permanent economic losses.

Duration is rarely viewed as a standalone risk factor, however it is nonetheless a key risk consideration. Many investors found out in 2022 that many "high-quality" assets such as municipal bonds delivered sharp losses to investors which are, if not permanent, likely long-term in nature.

Leverage is straight-forward – it magnifies the risk of a given asset, i.e., a leveraged loan CEF is higher-risk than an unleveraged loan CEF, all else equal. Whether leverage produces additional yield is not a given – in an environment of a flat yield curve and tight spreads, leverage generates little additional yield for most credit CEFs.

On the discount side – many investors know it in their bones that discount behavior is procyclical, i.e., they exacerbate up and down moves, so if the NAV drop is 5%, the price drop might be 7% due to additional discount widening and vice versa.

What this breakdown means is that on the lower-risk side of the barbell it’s a good idea to target 1) higher-credit quality sectors such as munis and preferreds, 2) assets with no explicit leverage such as individual bonds and preferreds (rather than, say, leveraged preferred CEFs) and 3) securities that don’t trade at discounts to NAV such as open-end funds, bonds and preferreds.

Duration is more complicated. Longer-duration / higher-credit quality assets such as munis and some preferreds can protect investors in a macro downturn when longer-term rates drop and credit spreads widen. However, they may not protect investors from an inflation shock, as many found out in 2022. For this reason, it's a good idea to have a balanced mix of longer-duration and shorter-duration assets. In the latter bucket, we would include floating-rate assets and bonds with a maturity of 2–3 years.

Can a barbell be built solely out of CEFs? It can be, however, it would miss out on the benefits of a full barbell portfolio so much that it would barely qualify as a barbell. Recall that a barbell requires not just assets from different sectors but with different risk characteristics to be optimized.

It's also important to highlight that there is a trade-off in a barbell portfolio. That trade-off has to do with yield on one hand and diversification / potential rotation / lower volatility on the other. A portfolio that is "all in" will tend to have a higher yield than a barbell portfolio since a barbell portfolio will, almost by definition, have assets with a lower yield.

A barbell portfolio, on the other hand, will tend to exhibit less volatility due to the lower-risk bucket. This will not only keep behavioral biases at bay (i.e., for example, the tendency to sell at the lows and buy back higher due to excessive portfolio drawdowns) but will also allow investors to take advantage of the drawdown by rotating from lower-risk assets to higher-yielding assets.

This last factor is an essential feature and a key benefit of the barbell. A barbell portfolio provides positive convexity by allowing the investor to rotate between the two barbell portions. The investor, in effect, sells high and buys low over time by having the freedom to move capital into the lower-risk portion when valuations are expensive and vice versa. This creates another source of return, allowing the investor to monetize high volatility.

Barbell Ideas

On the higher-risk / yield side of the barbell, we like a number of floating-rate assets, such as BDCs and CLO Equity securities. The sectors behind these assets can be very volatile, and they are exposed to credit losses in a recession. However, they also deliver high yields and allow investors to participate in strong market returns.

Some of our BDC holdings include:

  • Nuveen Churchill Direct Lending (NCDL), trading at a 10.3% yield and a 4% discount,
  • Bain Capital Specialty Finance (BCSF), trading 10.7% yield and a 5% discount,
  • Golub Capital BDC (GBDC), trading at a 10.2% yield and a 2% premium

On the CLO Equity side, we like:

  • CLO Equity / Loan CEF XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT), trading at a 15.5% yield and a 3% discount.
  • CLO Equity CEF Pearl Diver Credit Co (PDCC), trading at an 18.8% yield and a 4% discount

On the other side of the barbell we like a mix of open-end funds, individual preferreds and bonds.

  • Agency mortgage REIT Dynex Capital Series C (DX.PR.C), which will shortly shift to a near 10% yield when it floats in mid-April. The stock has the highest level of equity coverage in the sector and offers very strong risk/reward,
  • Municipal mutual fund First Eagle High Yield Municipal Fund (FEHAX) with a 4.9% distribution rate.
  • Baby bond from CLO Equity CEF OXLC (OXLCI), trading at an 8.2% yield.

Takeaways

A barbell portfolio has a number of advantages for income investors.

First, it provides quality diversification from the top down. A bottom-up approach to portfolio construction can often get away from investors, resulting in a portfolio that does not actually reflect the investor's overall risk appetite.

Secondly, it creates the potential for rebalancing and using the lower-risk portion of the portfolio as a source of "dry powder" to take advantage of attractive higher-yielding opportunities during market drawdowns.

Finally, a barbell approach mitigates the worst-case scenario outcome due to the presence of higher-quality assets in the portfolio.

Over 2024 we have mostly been reducing our allocation in the higher-risk portion of the barbell, most notably by reducing our CEF sleeve and rotating into individual preferreds and bonds. Tight credit spreads and discounts, as well as modest additional income on leveraged assets meant that the opportunity cost for being in higher-risk assets was minimal.

The recent market environment has been favorable for a barbell allocation approach. Risk sentiment has soured, which has increased volatility and dispersion in the market, opening up new opportunities. Credit spreads have also widened, which has pushed down the valuation of some of the more volatile sectors such as CLO Equity and opened up discounts in some CEFs and BDCs. We have taken advantage of this dynamic and have topped up the CEF/BDC portion of the barbell in the last month or two.

data.title: MasTec: A Good Buy Given Strong Backlog And Secular Tailwinds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

MasTec, Inc. (NYSE:MTZ) is well-positioned to benefit from its strong backlog, which offers good visibility for its near-term revenue growth. Further, there are several secular and regulatory growth drivers across its segments. In the Communications segment, the company should benefit from strong demand for telecom infrastructure and federal funding like the BEAD Program. Additionally, strength in the Clean Energy & Infrastructure segment, driven by megatrends like reshoring, data center work, and the need for energy independence, and secular demand drivers in the Power Delivery segment, including grid modernization, should support revenue growth. While the Pipeline Infrastructure segment faces near-term headwinds from the completion of the Mountain Valley Pipeline project, the strength in other segments should more than offset this headwind, and the segment's medium- to long-term prospects are attractive with growing demand for gas-fired power generation catalyzing gas pipeline investment. Besides organic growth, the company has a healthy balance sheet and is well placed to do bolt-on M&As.

On the margin front, the company should benefit from operating leverage on higher revenues and increased workforce utilization. Further, better project pricing and the company's focus on project and customer selection should support margin expansion. In terms of valuation, the stock is trading at a discount to its historical averages. Given the good growth prospects and lower-than-historical valuation, I have a buy rating on MTZ stock.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

MTZ has seen good growth in recent years, supported by secular and regulatory tailwinds across its end markets, including increased investments in 5G infrastructure, sustainability megatrend, transition to clean energy, increased requirement of investment in power transmission, and the need for energy independence. The company has reported its Q4 2024 results, and similar dynamics were seen there.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company's revenue grew by 3.7% Y/Y to $3.403 billion. On a segment basis, the Communications segment's revenues increased by 28.3% Y/Y, driven by increased levels of wireless, wireline, and utility project activity.

In the Clean Energy and Infrastructure segment, revenues were up 17.8% Y/Y, attributed to growth across renewables, infrastructure, and industrial verticals.

In the Power Delivery segment, revenues increased by 15.8% Y/Y as a result of increased project activity, including transmission and substation-related project work.

Lastly, in the Pipeline Infrastructure segment, the revenue declined by 46.5% Y/Y due to the reduced large-diameter project activity and the completion of the Mountain Valley Pipeline (MVP) project, which benefited revenue in Q4 2023.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's growth prospects.

The company reported a total backlog of $14.3 billion at the end of last quarter, which reflected an increase of $440 million sequentially and $1.9 billion Y/Y growth. A company's backlog is a leading indicator of its revenues, and healthy backlog growth bodes well for the company's growth in the coming quarters.

The company has good near-term as well as long-term growth drivers for its three biggest segments: Communications, Clean Energy and Infrastructure, and Power Delivery.

In the Communications segment, the company is poised to benefit from strong demand for telecom infrastructure, which is expanding both in terms of geography and services. The funding and build-out of broadband infrastructure supported by federal investment programs like BEAD is another growth driver for the segment. The demand created by hyperscalers is also driving long-haul buildout activity, which should continue to support demand for the next several years as generative AI continues to gain traction. The company won a contract from Lumen last quarter, and management noted that they are in late-stage discussions with customers on several projects, which should help backlog and revenue growth moving forward.

In the Clean Energy and Infrastructure Segment, the company is seeing strong demand in renewables, infrastructure, and industrial sectors, with energy independence, rebuilding America's infrastructure, data center work, and reshoring being the key underlying drivers. The company is seeing significant opportunities related to data center activities in this segment and realized $200 million in revenue from these activities in 2024. For FY25, it is targeting around $300 million in revenue from data center-related work.

In the Power Delivery segment, the company should benefit from the startup of the Greenlink transmission project, which is expected to contribute between $300 and $500 million in revenues in 2025. The favorable resolution of rate case appeals for two Midwest utility clients should also result in increased CapEx by them, helping MTZ's revenue. From a long-term perspective, there is a significant opportunity from grid modernization as utilities focus on meeting increased demand for power and grid reliability.

One segment that is seeing some weakness is the Pipeline Infrastructure segment. This is mainly due to the completion of the Mountain Valley Pipeline project, which is expected to impact 2025 revenues. However, the strength in other segments should more than offset this weakness in the near term. From a medium- to long-term perspective, the increasing demand for gas-fired power generation requires investment in pipeline infrastructure and should help the segment's revenue growth to turn positive in the coming years.

In addition to good organic growth prospects, the company has a healthy balance sheet with a net leverage of 1.8x, which positions it well for bolt-on M&As.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q4 2024, the company's margins benefited from operating leverage on higher revenues and good project execution. This resulted in a 90 bps Y/Y expansion in the total adjusted EBITDA margin to 8%.

Segment-wise, the adjusted EBITDA margin expanded by 230 bps Y/Y in the Communications and 350 bps Y/Y in the Clean Energy & Infrastructure segments. Despite Y/Y revenue decline, the Pipeline Infrastructure segment's adjusted EBITDA margin increased by 170 bps Y/Y primarily due to improved project efficiencies and favorable project mix from the absence of the MVP project, which was dilutive to margins. However, in the Power Delivery segment, the adjusted EBITDA margin was down 90 bps Y/Y.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. A healthy revenue outlook means the company should benefit from operating leverage and better utilization of the workforce. A good demand environment should also enable the company to secure better pricing on projects, which should help margins.

Management also shared their focus on project and customer selection to align with their expected return profile. So, I am expecting a better margin mix moving forward. MasTec's size and scale also differentiate it versus smaller peers, and it is well-positioned to handle large-size projects with increasing complexity, which should enable it to charge better pricing.

Valuation & Conclusion

MTZ stock is trading at 21.81x FY25 consensus EPS estimate of $5.63 and 18.10x FY26 EPS estimate of $6.78, which is lower than its 5-year average forward P/E of 23.26x.

The stock is also trading at a discount versus its peer Quanta Services (PWR), which is trading at 25.96x FY25 consensus EPS estimates and 22.66x FY26 consensus EPS estimates, despite MTZ having higher consensus EPS growth estimates for the current year and the next.

I believe the company has good growth potential over the coming years. Revenue growth should benefit from a strong backlog and secular and regulatory tailwinds across its end markets, including strong demand from telecom infrastructure, federal funding under the BEAD program, megatrends like the reshoring, grid modernization, energy independence, and growing demand for gas-fired power generation. In addition, inorganic growth opportunities from M&As also support the revenue outlook. The margin outlook is also favorable, with benefits from operating leverage and better project pricing and mix. The valuation is also cheap compared to its historical averages. Given the good growth prospects and cheap valuation, I have a buy rating on MTZ stock.

Risks

In addition to organic growth, the company also follows a bolt-on M&A strategy to improve growth. Inorganic growth is relatively riskier than organic growth, and there are always risks related to integration missteps, overpaying for an acquisition, and the leverage a company takes to make an acquisition. In case any future acquisition goes wrong or the management fails to realize the anticipated benefits of an acquisition, it may negatively impact the stock price.

The recently announced tariffs may result in cost inflation across the board in the U.S. economy and for the company. While a good demand environment is expected to help the company implement pricing increases to offset it, if the company is not able to implement pricing to offset inflationary headwinds, its margins may suffer.

data.title: Bitcoin: Unexpected Portfolio Optimization Benefits
data.description:
It's been approximately three years since we last weighed in on Bitcoin (BTC-USD), where we argued that certain correlations would lead to sustained momentum. Although it has suffered from a few bumps ever since, Bitcoin has delivered index-beating returns, consequently verifying our analysis to a degree.

Today's report approaches Bitcoin from a different angle, whereby we re-assess the cryptocurrency's optics in context of its maturing nature and its portfolio optimization benefits.

Without further delay, here's a summary of what we discovered.

Bitcoin: Price Evaluation & A Maturing 'Asset Class'

Market Commentary

Bitcoin has delivered solid returns in the past year, after slumping during the initial stages of global interest rate increases and the concurrent bear market. Despite the coin's resurgence, a slight decrease has occurred since the turn of the year, likely due to a broad-based decline in risky stocks and a technical correction.

So, looking ahead, what's in-store for Bitcoin? First, let's run through a correlation matrix to establish Bitcoin's correlation parameters.

A time series correlation matrix shows that Bitcoin's correlation to major asset classes has strengthened in recent years with its correlation to the Russell 1000 increasing to 0.62 between 2020 and 2024. However, interestingly enough, Bitcoin's correlation to the dollar has inverted even further, settling at -0.22 for the period between 2020 and 2024; moreover, Bitcoin's correlation to the VIX has stayed negative, at least until 2023. Collectively, these figures make for an interesting analysis due to a potential shift in influencing variables.

Adding to the above, it's shown that Bitcoin's price volatility has diminished in recent years. While still a volatile asset, Bitcoin's current volatility is on-par with certain big tech stocks, which seemed unthinkable a decade ago. In our view, Bitcoin is maturing, which is both statistically evident and qualitatively explainable (elaborated upon later).

Herewith is a rolling volatility correlation to substantiate some of the claims made in the preceding paragraph.

Maturity

Institutional Acceptance

Brian Moynihan spoke earlier this year, stating that “If the rules come in and make it a real thing that you can actually do business with, you’ll find that the banking system will come in hard on the transactional side of it,” - Moynihan's words provide a leading-indicator of Bitcoin's institutional level acceptance, especially given the Trump Administration's (and various other global political parties) pro-Bitcoin stance. Moreover, index funds have capitalized on the demand for Bitcoin as an investable asset vehicle; in our view, indexation could add tremendous gravitational pull.

Furthermore, financial market participants often overlook Bitcoin's transactional value (it's not merely an investable asset). Sure, Bitcoin might not be backed by tax dollars; however, it has tremendous real-world application. Until now, most of the transactional utility has occurred in developed or top-tier emerging nations. However, we think the secret sauce lies in emerging markets where people with volatile currencies might want to utilize their Bitcoin reserves as a means to protect against volatile soft currencies - I speak out of experience.

Potential Proxy For Gold?

I pointed to Bitcoin's negative USD correlation earlier in the article. Investors have historically opted for Gold as a USD hedge, especially whenever tail risk emerged. Although speculative, Bitcoin could be used as a co-reserve alongside gold, providing similar principles with diversification benefits.

Here's a recent state street study discussing how Gold and Bitcoin could arguably be used together in the future: Link Here

Portfolio Optimization

Portfolio optimization is primarily a statistical study emphasizing risk-adjusted return maximization through diversified portfolios. While an observation of standard deviations and correlations might possess limitations in the real world, they provide a valuable guideline to investors with an interest in objective theories as opposed to emotional decision-making.

We selected a few portfolio methods to illustrate Bitcoin's potential application in portfolio optimization. More specifically, we studied heuristic portfolios, including the 60/40 and 1/N. Moreover, we looked at a Global Market Portfolio equivalent, to add width to the analysis by including a broad range of asset classes.

Here's a summary of some of the terms used in the analysis.

  • Sharpe Ratio: A measure of risk-adjusted returns by observing excess returns relative to volatility.
  • Sortino Ratio: Similar to the Sharpe ratio. However, this metric bases its denominator on downside deviation. In addition, it sets its excess return benchmark equivalent to predefined investors' required return instead of a risk-free asset.
  • Information Ratio: Measures excess return relative to tracking error by basing both against a benchmark.
  • Efficient Frontier: A series of optimal portfolios based on investors' desired risk-return appetite.
  • Tangency Portfolio: The point on the efficient frontier that maximizes the selected risk-adjusted return metric.
  • 60/40 Heuristic: A heuristic approach to asset allocation, comprising of 60% stocks and 40% bonds. As investors age, this method often turns into other approaches, such as 120 minus age.
  • 1/N Heuristic: A selection of major asset classes weighted equally to ensure broad-based exposure while minimizing the time and effort related to managing broad-based exposure.
  • Global Market Portfolio: A portfolio that aims to sample all investable assets by market capitalization.
  • ETF Proxies: Thematic vehicle proxies used to sample the underlying asset exposure of each portfolio methodology.

During the testing process, we emphasized the maximum Sharpe Ratios and Information Ratios. Other methodologies can be used; however, portfolio optimization via Sharpe and IR is quite common in practice.

60/40 Heuristic

The 60/40 is probably one of the most common heuristic portfolios, ideally suited to investors who seek practical yet effective solutions. Here's a view of the portfolio's weights, its ETF proxies, as well as portfolio weights for the maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio.

Fund Equal Weight 60/40 Max SR
Vanguard Russell 3000 (VTHR) 50% 60% 100%
Vanguard Total Bond Market (BND) 50% 40% 0%

Source: Author's Work

Here's the regression's results, a discussion follows.

  • A pure equity portfolio maximizes the Sharpe and Sortino Ratios.
  • A pure equity portfolio maximizes expected return.
  • A 50/50 portfolio minimizes maximum drawdowns.

In essence, you'll likely experience superior risk-adjusted returns from a pure equity portfolio. However, market tail risk could be more severe, meaning a lack of asset class diversification might lead to an undesirable maximum drawdown. Thus, allocation would depend on an investor's risk-return preference.

1/N

Here's the data for a 1/N portfolio. Kindly note that the selected major asset classes were at our discretion; investors' definition of major asset classes might differ.

ETF/Fund 1/N Max SR
Vanguard Russell 3000 14.29% 45.39%
Vanguard Total Bond Market 14.29% -
iShares Core MSCI EAFE (IEFA) 14.28% -
Vanguard FTSE Emerging Markets (VWO) 14.28% -
iShares iBoxx $ Invmt Grade Corp Bd (LQD) 14.29% -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 14.28% -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 14.29% 54.61%

Source: Author's Work - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

Herewith are the results; a discussion follows.

The results of the 1/N provide novelty in relation to the 60/40; here are the key findings.

  • The Sharpe Ratio and Sortino Ratio are both lower for the 1/N portfolio than for the 60/40 and pure equity portfolios.
  • Despite the 1/N's shortcomings, an altered 1/N shows that we can achieve superior Sharpe and Sortino ratios by restricting certain asset classes from the 1/N.
  • The expected return on the restricted 1/N is slightly lower than the pure equity portfolio, placing it second thus far. However, its maximum drawdown is the lowest of all the portfolios tested until this point-in-time.
  • The maximum Sharpe Ratio achievable provides an unbalance portfolio, significantly overweighing Gold and Stocks while neglecting broader diversification. However, despite its shortcomings, the portfolio's maximum drawdown is lower than the preceding diversified portfolios.

Global Market Portfolio

As with the 1/N, the GMP's allocation can be subjective. For example, asset class representation might be mis-sampled and subject to fluctuation. Furthermore, asset accessibility might be restricted, and proxy ETFs usually have partial correlations to the targeted asset classes as opposed to near perfect correlations.

Before presenting our selected liquid asset weights, I'd like to introduce you to a GMP created by State Street in late 2024. The diagram provides an idea of the GMP's exposure.

Next up, a table showing the portfolio's liquid vehicle weights.

Aside: BIZD was used to represent private credit, which is a distant proxy, yet the closest we could find. Additionally, certain liquid vehicles provide amalgamated exposure to asset classes, meaning their testing weights differ from the GMP's.

ETF/Fund GMP Max SR Max IR
Vanguard Total World Stock (VT) 44.8% 23.34% 61.17%
Vanguard Total International Bond (BNDX) 29.8% 11.67% -
iShares MBS (MBB) 4.8% -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 5.8% -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 4.2% 25% 20.85%
Invesco Global Listed Private Equity (PSP) 3% -
iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust (GSG) 3% 3.57% -
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 2% 6.41% 8.02%
SPDR Blmbg Convert Secs (CWB) 1.5% 25% 9.96%
iShares Short Treasury Bond (SHV) 1.1% 5% -

Source: Author's Work - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

Above are the weights for our GMP portfolio, below are the results; a discussion follows suit.

The initial maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio notably overweighed non-core asset classes. As a result, we configured a high Sharpe Ratio but to the detriment of other metrics. To correct the bias, we added asset constraints, whereby we incorporated a maximum information ratio strategy and weighting restrictions.

Here's a breakdown of the weighting restrictions.

  • Stocks and Bonds capped at 50% of the portfolio's allocatable amount.
  • Other core assets such as Gold and Real Estate capped at 25%.
  • Non-core asset classes capped at 2x their weight in the GMP.

Here are the results we discovered.

  • None of the tested portfolios contest the restricted 1/N max Sharpe Ratio portfolio in terms of Sharpe Ratio.
  • The restricted 1/N portfolio still holds superior expected return.
  • The pure equity portfolio in the 60/40 analysis reigns supreme with regards to expected returns.
  • The maximum drawdown is minimized through the GMP's maximum Sharpe Ratio portfolio.

By investing in all investable assets, we see docile numbers. The portfolio can be adjusted to maximize the Sharpe Ratio, which results in lower maximum drawdown and a best-in-class Sortino Ratio. The optimal allocation within this dataset suggests that it might suit low-risk, medium return-seeking investors but fails to deliver optimal risk-adjusted returns.

Adding Bitcoin

Maximizing Metrics Holistically

We tested a theory, which added Bitcoin to the GMP, inclusive of the 60/40, and 1/N heuristic portfolios. Similar to the previous section, we set constraints on maximum allocations to optimize metrics throughout, instead of merely isolating the Sharpe Ratio.

Herewith are the weights that were used.

ETF/Fund GMP Max SR Max IR
Vanguard Total World Stock (VT) 44.8% 33.89% 50%
Vanguard Total International Bond (BNDX) 29.8% 5.91% -
iShares MBS (MBB) 4.8% - -
Vanguard Real Estate (VNQ) 5.8% - -
SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) 4.2% 25% 25%
Invesco Global Listed Private Equity (PSP) 3% - 4.39%
iShares S&P GSCI Commodity-Indexed Trust (GSG) 3% 6% -
VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) 2% 4% 4%
SPDR Blmbg Convert Secs (CWB) 1.5% 3% 3%
iShares Short Treasury Bond (SHV) 1.1% 2.2% -
Bitcoin 0% 20% 13.61%

Source: Author In Portfolio Visualizer - Might Be Subject To Small Rounding Errors

The results are conveyed in the next diagram, a discussion follows.

As with the GMP test, we applied the maximum Information Ratio portfolio test to supplement the Sharpe Ratio portfolio. Moreover, we applied constraints to the optimization, introducing focus on all the key ratios instead of merely the Sharpe and Information Ratios.

Here are the constraints.

  • Stocks and Bonds capped at 50% of the portfolio's allocatable amount.
  • Other core assets such as Gold and Real Estate capped at 25%.
  • Non-core asset classes capped at 2x their weight in the GMP.
  • Bitcoin capped at 20%.

Here are the results we discovered.

  • We were able to maximize the Sharpe Ratios within both the GMP + Bitcoin's max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios to levels exceeding all preceding tests.
  • We were able to maximize the Sortino Ratios within both the GMP + Bitcoin's max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios to levels exceeding all preceding tests.
  • Aside from maximum drawdown, all metrics outshined the VBIAX ETF (VBIAX), which is an ETF used as a 60/40 portfolio benchmark proxy.
  • The Maximum Drawdowns increased significantly in both the both of the GMP + Bitcoin max Sharpe Ratio and Information Ratio portfolios. However, as illustrated in the next section, lower higher-risk asset exposure, including Bitcoin, lowered maximum drawdown significantly while maintaining high Sharpe Ratios.

Efficient Frontier

The information ratio-based efficient frontier shows that the GMP + Bitcoin portfolio can be used to reach the tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier, which includes Bitcoin exposure of approximately 13.61%.

Aside: The maximum information ratio weights used in the introduction equates to the tangency portfolio weights.

Furthermore, lower risk investors can benefit from Bitcoin exposure. The efficient frontier shows that portfolios with 0.15% active returns, yet positive information ratios load approximately 1.6% of Bitcoin in the portfolio, indicating that Bitcoin has application in lower risk portfolio optimization.

From a volatility relative perspective, the Sharpe Ratio shows that Bitcoin plays a key role in optimization with the tangency portfolio achieved by allocating 13.61% to Bitcoin.

Although optimized for volatility-adjusted returns, the tangency portfolio might onboard more risk than some might be willing to. However, a more docile portfolio with a standard deviation of 7.91% (versus the S&P 500's 33-y average of 15.20%) shows that Bitcoin still plays a material role in achieving annualized returns of above 10%.

Risks

Other Metrics Should Be considered

Only a handful of metrics were considered in the analysis. Although the metrics are salient to risk-return analysis. Additional metrics such as value-at-risk, non-linear correlation coefficients, skewness, and kurtosis might add value to a holistic analysis.

"It's Backed By Nothing"

Many, including Jamie Dimon and Warren Buffett, have argued that Bitcoin is unsustainable. Moreover, many retail investors and mainstream actors tend to argue against Bitcoin as an investment on the basis of it being "backed by nothing." Whether counter-arguments hold validity or not is debatable; however, it would be naive to ignore the opinion of opponents as financial markets comprises of buyers and sellers, not just buyers.

Recent Hacking

To our understanding, initially, it was argued that Bitcoin's novelty was largely due to its black box status, presenting a sense of security to holders. However, as many might be aware, numerous crypto wallets hacking have occurred in recent times, raising question marks about the notion of Bitcoin being a safety zone to privacy-seeking holders.

Historical Returns Change With Time (Conditioning)

The regression was calibrated over a period of less than ten years, largely due to historical data limitations. Although insights are valuable, society, the economy, and financial market behavior changes as time passes, therefore limiting the validity of historical simulation analysis.

Concluding Thoughts

Our portfolio optimization tests suggest that Bitcoin could be used as an instrument to enhance risk-adjusted returns. Although limited in time-frame and dependable on the past, the regression analysis communicates valuable inferences to those interested in adjusting their portfolios to modern financial market behavior.

As for Bitcoin's interim price direction. We acknowledge that its short-term predictability is limited, and therefore we refrained from committing to a short-term price discovery opinion. However, we are in favor of Bitcoin's long-term prospects, given its growing societal acceptance, recent institutional uptake, and its general behavior as an 'asset class' - we retain our 'buy' rating.

Kindly Note: The article was authored and tested throughout the space of a week. Therefore, the data may have altered slightly between the time the report was constructed and published.

data.title: SMH: Falling Like Rain, But It's Time To Embrace The Pain
data.description:
What a ride for semiconductor investors this week, as President Trump sets in motion his reciprocal tariffs, as the US government is determined to remodel the global economic system threatening to upend the globalized trading relationships into one that arguably favors American protectionism.

Yet, Trumpians might argue that such a reordering may have been long overdue, as Trump unleashed a tirade against the so-called "globalists," and it doesn’t seem to matter whether you are considered adversarial or allied to America’s interests, as friends and foes were slapped with reciprocal tariffs that could pave the way for an unprecedented global trade war unseen in the modern era.

Such global ramifications have already sent shockwaves across the US equities markets, and also the dollar, as investors bailed out of even the best companies like the Magnificent Seven, with seemingly little regard about their fundamentals, including some leading semiconductor companies listed in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH).

In just over two months, my previous SMH update seems dated, as we face new emerging risks that may not just embroil the US economy, businesses, and consumers, but also implicate the global economy as they double down on their negotiations to try to make Trump change his mind and reverse or markedly lower the punitive tariffs that he has imposed on their trading relationship.

Would it happen? I believe your guess is as good as mine now, as Trump’s policy whiplash and predilection for making last-minute changes or decisions suggest even the most well-thought-out analysis or forecasts could be “subject to changes” that render them irrelevant at a moment’s notice. Such unpredictability will not just stonewall business decision-making, but also threaten to push back on downstream enterprise spending while threatening to compel consumers to cut back further on discretionary purchases of semiconductor products if they think a recession is imminent.

While the hard data may not yet show the “lagged effects” of Trump’s tariffs percolating through the global economy, the market’s response with sharp selloffs engulfing growth and tech stocks is a stark reminder that investors are taking risks off the table at a pace not seen for some time.

If there is a silver lining per se, I believe it’s that Trump hasn’t updated us (yet) on whether he will impose semi tariffs, or when he intends to do it. I’m not going to try and second guess him as that’s a futile attempt.

Yet, although semiconductor tariffs have yet to be announced, the effects from the wide-ranging and significant tariffs on the global trading ecosystem are expected to hamper the well-oiled semiconductor supply chain that has generally benefited American fabless chipmakers, while they relied on chipmaking, repackaging and contract manufacturing partners in Asia for the lower margin processes.

Hence, we already see intense selling across the semi hardware stocks, while chipmakers and chip designers have also not been spared. Chip equipment suppliers are also in the crosshairs as the market starts to price in an increasing likelihood of recession as a base case, suggesting multiples compression and significant cuts in earnings estimates are now being contemplated by analysts and investors.

As for the semiconductor value chain which has arguably enjoyed a “division of labor” and comparative advantage of sorts (US designs, while Asia produces), that relationship that has arguably defined the semiconductor industry is set to change in a big, big way.

Make no mistake about it. I think something is brewing underneath the surface between the Trump administration and the semiconductor companies, as America moves from offering subsidies through the CHIPS Act (initiated by former President Biden) to using tariffs as the main negotiating leverage to “compel” chipmakers to reshore their production and repackaging capacity in a hurry to fulfill Trump’s vision of restructuring America’s chip value chain.

As a reminder, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) has already given us a prelude to what Trump is looking for when the Taiwanese foundry formalized and announced its decision to invest another $100B in early March, adding to the already massive $65M commitment in the US.

And, the call for TSMC to invest or help Intel foundry stand up proudly again has regained traction, as the media reported TSMC considering investing a 20% stake in Intel Corporation (INTC), which might also involve “sharing some of its chipmaking methods with Intel,” instead of taking a financial stake.

Does that mean Taiwan Semi is contemplating a "technology transfer" or sorts to Intel? Does it mean that Intel can finally untether its loss-making chipmaking division to TSMC, although questions about significant retrenchments with such a deal could foment resistance within the company.

However, with a new CEO on board, the chances of it happening are looking increasingly likely, as Lip-Bu Tan highlighted that he’s keen on shedding “non-core” assets, although it’s not immediately clear what he meant by that. Still, Intel appears to be making constructive progress with its 18A node, suggesting it might have a realistic chance of closing the gap with TSMC’s tech, although yields and production reliability remain uncertain.

Could Taiwan’s 32% reciprocal tariffs rate have played a role in the Taiwanese government possibly helping to broker such a deal, but could also reduce the strength of the “silicon shield” if America “coerces” a bigger shift of more advanced chip manufacturing capacity away from Taiwan.

And how might it affect the earnings profile of TSMC and even its downstream chip designer peers? Coupled with the complexities emanating from Trump’s Liberation Day, I believe the need for NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA) and its AI peers to drum up support for the AI growth story is increasingly pressing than ever. As the top two constituents (TSMC and Nvidia) in SMH, it’s imperative for investors to pay close attention to their ongoing developments and challenges.

And there are indeed mixed feelings here, I think. While Nvidia shows at its Spring GTC that all seems to be well for Blackwell as it ramps, the significant price declines for its Hopper chips rental have also raised the specter of the perils of getting stuffed with outmoded AI chips, as Nvidia continues its annualized new product generation cadence to maintain its tech edge over its peers.

Hence, mixed signals from Microsoft Corporation (MSFT) recently on its data center build-out have coincided with the IPO of CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), as the tier-two hyperscaler shows that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is reportedly keen to double down on its AI progress to close the gap on the leading AI companies.

Moreover, SoftBank Group Corp.'s (OTCPK:SFTBY) confidence in funding the grand ambitions of OpenAI corroborates that the AI growth inflection doesn’t seem to be petering out. And while Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) Chair Joe Tsai cautioned about an impending AI bubble, Chinese tech companies have continued to place orders with Nvidia for H20 AI chips, demonstrating that we might need to look beyond mere narrative to assess the underlying momentum driving the AI revolution.

However, other exigent headwinds could still dampen investor sentiments in SMH. Aside from the ambiguous chip tariffs, the AI diffusion rules are set to come into effect sometime in May 2025. The Commerce Department hasn’t provided enough clues into anticipated modifications, although we should expect the rules could be tightened to further deny China access to the most cutting-edge AI chips.

Hence, these could also render a dilemma for semi investors thinking about “jumping the gun” to buy more SMH exposure now, even as they navigate a minefield of potential downward earnings revisions that have already dodged the industry for the past few months.

Let me be clear here. There should be little doubt that the selling intensity has eviscerated much optimism, to say the least for semiconductor investors. However, there are also signs of a possible capitulation as SMH re-tests its 52-week lows.

So even though the S&P 500 has not dropped into a bear market, the same cannot be said for semiconductor stocks, as we felt the brunt of the selloff, taking a substantial toll as growth and tech stocks were roiled.

Yet, amid the conspicuous pessimism, a successful re-test here could see dip-buyers returning, and the long-term trend bias for SMH remains skewed to the upside. In other words, unless you believe that there’s a structural downshift in the fundamentals of semiconductor stocks, the opportunity to take a more aggressive posture in SMH may be close.

Of course, catching the falling knife may not be something that all investors are attuned to, but it’s certainly something that I’ve been accustomed to. That being said, if you need further clarification into the bullish reversal tendencies, then, perhaps biding your time for a price action validation could be worthwhile, as a failure at the current levels could entail further downside, suggesting that a recessionary P/E must be contemplated.

While the semiconductor industry’s forward PEG has arguably dropped below 1, the forward growth rates could be de-rated in favor of more conservative forecasts to rein in the previous optimism if the market thinks that Trump is determined not to offer any sorts of concessions to the tariffs, although he has also highlighted he remains open to “phenomenal deals.”

With trading nations arguably still in the negotiation phase, I expect things to happen “fast and furious.” Yet, the selling intensity in SMH has reached feverish depths, suggesting a bottom could form over the next few weeks.

While I don’t have a crystal ball, I believe the opportunity to take further exposure in SMH appears to be apt, and 90% of its exposure is rated with at least a narrow economic moat, corroborating favorably for investors who only want to invest in quality during these turbulent times.

Rating: Maintain Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tariffs, Dilution, And Decline: The Bear Case For PDD Holdings
data.description:
It’s evident that China shares have been taking a breather in recent weeks after the relentless early-year rally – especially in the Chinese tech and Internet sectors. The gradual decline in key gauges for the cohort (KWEB/KTEC) has reached as much as -13%+ over the past two weeks. Although it’s yet to look like a structural correction, the downtrend suggests one certain reality – profit-taking is in full swing, especially amidst ongoing tariff negotiations. And with U.S. equities looking more attractive following the first quarter selloff, it’s likely that “someone’s gotta give” from China’s overcrowded recovery rally.

We believe PDD Holdings Inc. (PDD) stands out as a particularly vulnerable name against the broad-based volatility observed across Chinese shares in recent weeks. Although the online bargain marketplace operator is often touted as China’s e-commerce “dark horse,” a deeper dive into PDD’s business model and fundamentals may suggest otherwise. From slowing growth to emerging dilution risks and looming tariff uncertainties, PDD stands on a weak foundation amidst shifting investor sentiment.

1. Losing Resonance with Consumers

PDD’s long been known for its competitive advantage in e-commerce due to the platform’s prescient focus on underserved opportunities in rural areas of China. Its “group buy” discounts and bargain deals offered through the local “Pinduoduo” platform have been a boon amongst inherently price sensitive consumers – especially those in lower tier economies across China. And by digitizing day-to-day grocery transactions and their logistics via “Duoduo Maicai,” PDD’s also offered rural farmers a chance to optimally monetize their harvest.

By carving out its niche in the underpenetrated corners of China's e-commerce, PDD’s successfully built a moat for itself. This has been key to preserving its growth against competition from bigger rivals like Alibaba’s (BABA) Taobao and Tmall, and JD.com (JD).

But even with 59% y/y growth delivered in FY24, PDD’s failed to appease investors’ aggressive appetite. The continuous deceleration observed over the past year highlights management’s acknowledgement of rapidly evolving consumer dynamics that appears to be a structural headwind for PDD’s business model. Due to the post-pandemic economic downturn and property slump, Chinese consumers have actually increased their appetite for “quality and personalization,” rather than wasting money on “cheap thrills” that PDD’s long been known for.

And Beijing’s recent policy support for consumption has only worsened the situation for PDD. The trade-in subsidies extended by the central government have so far only benefitted premium goods, such as home appliances and consumer electronics – segments dominated by Alibaba’s Tmall and JD.com. Despite PDD’s efforts in building its premium goods catalogue, the platform’s yet to rid its reputation as a marketplace for bargains that’s typically matched with a “you get what you pay for” low-level customer experience.

It's evident that PDD’s losing the moat it’s built on consumers’ inherent preference for affordability and bargains. And moving away from this legacy business model could require a costly operational overhaul. Despite having already initiated a ¥10 billion (~$1.4 billion) fee reduction program that’s aimed at incentivizing quality improvements across merchants, PDD’s revenue growth has remained in deceleration. This highlights the insufficiency in related investments given the depth of its brand mismatch with consumer preferences.

And to address consumers’ increasing demand for quality goods, the company’s also going to have to dive knee-deep into competition with Alibaba and JD.com’s respective platforms as well. This essentially introduces accelerating growth risks ahead for PDD, which shouldn’t be dismissed.

2. Shaky Margins

Although PDD’s Q4 revenue missed estimates, it had still managed to outperform earnings. This was a consistent trend observed in each of the preceding quarters as well – PDD had outperformed FY24 EPADS estimates by 100%+, despite repeated revenue misses during the year.

But a deeper dive into the drivers would yield deterring revelations. The company’s gross margins had actually deteriorated by two percentage points from the prior year, dropping from 63% in FY23 to 61% in FY24. And the consecutive quarterly earnings outperformances through FY24 have primarily been driven by “below-the-line” items generated outside of core operations, which aren’t sustainable. Specifically, below-the-line items grew multiple times faster than PDD’s pace of revenue expansion and operating leverage improvements in FY24, with the more-than-doubled interest and investment income having been a major driver. This offers limited durability to PDD’s earnings quality, as improvements in below-the-line items do not provide reinforcement to the underlying business’ organic scalability and optimization.

And circling back to observations of gross margin deterioration at PDD, the situation is unlikely to ameliorate anytime soon. In PDD’s latest earnings press release, management had highlighted higher fulfillment and payment processing fees as the key driver of gross margin declines in FY24. This is likely linked to the ¥10 billion fee reduction initiative extended to merchants, which aims at incentivizing them to reinvest cost-savings into higher quality products for end-market consumers. Given the initiative has yet to make an evident impact on its topline, it's likely PDD faces the need for further investments of this sort, which introduces protracted margin compression risks.

3. Tariff Uncertainties

The Trump tariffs are also going to be Temu’s Achilles’ Heel.

Although PDD does not disclose its revenue by platform nor geographic region, the company’s growth in recent years was likely primarily driven by Temu – PDD’s bargain platform overseas, primarily in the U.S. This is corroborated by PDD’s ballooning transaction services segment revenues, which are primarily generated from sales on Temu and Duoduo Maicai.

Since Temu’s launch in September 2022, transaction services revenue has grown by more than six-fold. And given the limited change observed in China’s local groceries demand environment, and PDD’s early-stage expansion to the country’s Western region, it’s unlikely that Duoduo Maicai’s been a key growth driver for the segment. This suggests Temu’s been the primary growth driver for PDD in recent years, which makes the company more prone to adverse implications of the ongoing trade war imposed by the U.S.

Specifically, upcoming changes to the “de minimis” exemption is expected to levy a harder impact on PDD than its other e-commerce peers like Alibaba. This is largely due to PDD’s outsized U.S. revenue exposure through Temu. Beginning May 2, any shipment from China to the U.S. that’s valued at or under $800 will be subject to either a 30% levy or a per-item charge of $25 (upped to $50 after June 1). This will inevitably weaken Temu’s accretive impact on PDD’s financials going forward. With the average order value on Temu currently estimated at just under $40, the upcoming charges are likely to deter purchases significantly.

And although PDD’s sought to mitigate the impact by setting up inventory in U.S. warehouses, it remains exposed to the aggressive tariffs that Trump’s announced earlier this week. Specifically, Trump’s announced a 54% tariff on Chinese imports on Liberation Day. The figure consists of a 20% levy imposed in March plus another 34% reciprocal top-up. And the outlook for relief appears bleak, given China’s just followed suit in retaliation by slapping a 34% levy on all U.S. imports starting April 10.

As a result, we remain incrementally cautious over compounding growth and margin pressures, given significant disruptions to PDD’s business model both at home and overseas.

4. Underappreciated Share Dilution Risk

Another overlooked, yet imminent, risk facing PDD is the looming maturity of ¥5.3 billion in convertible bonds due in December.

Recall that the company had issued $2 billion 0.00% convertible senior notes in November 2020. While the notes are set to mature on December 1, 2025, certain holders have elected to redeem about 63% of the outstanding amount for cash on December 1, 2023. This leaves about $750 million in convertible notes due coming in December at a conversion rate of 5.2459 ADS per $1,000 principal amount.

If converted, this could result in the issuance of around four million new American Depository Shares (“ADS”). This may seem negligible against PDD’s ~1.5 million diluted ADS count. But in a market environment that’s already strained by profit-taking and macroeconomic uncertainty, even nominal dilution can have an outsized impact on investor sentiment and confidence in the stock – especially given PDD’s underlying fundamental weakness.

Another concern is that PDD has yet to disclose any anti-dilution mechanisms, such as capped calls, for its outstanding convertible notes. This leaves limited mitigation against the impact on existing shareholders, which means if conversion occurs, the dilution will be felt in full. While PDD does have the cash to redeem the convertible notes when they become due, the ensuing reduction to its checkbook would inevitably dent its valuation as well. Either way, this is an imminent downside risk to consider for existing and prospective PDD investors.

Valuation Considerations

Admittedly, PDD appears to be trading at a deep discount to peers given its outsized (albeit decelerating) pace of growth. The stock is currently valued at about 2.6x NTM sales, which is expected to grow at 21%. That’s only a slight premium to Alibaba’s 2.1x, which is priced on NTM revenue growth of a mere 6%. But when considering an apples-to-apples comparison against Chinese e-commerce pureplay JD.com, PDD’s trading at an almost 9x premium versus its rival’s 0.3x NTM sales priced on ~8% FY25 growth. This triangular comparison suggests that Alibaba may be priced at a premium given its leading participation in higher growth AI opportunities; and PDD’s likely already trading at a level representative of investors’ appetite for its underlying operational risks.

This is consistent with observations in valuation multiples that depend on earnings and cash flow projections. Specifically, PDD is currently trading at about 6.8x NTM EV/EBITDA and 9.9x NTM earnings. This compares to 9.0x and 13.5x, respectively, at Alibaba, and 6.3x and 8.7x, respectively, at JD.com. The modest premium over JD.com and discount to Alibaba is likely attributable to PDD’s relatively higher risks of margin decay and elevated earnings dependence on unsustainable below-the-line items.

Conclusion

On the surface, PDD’s modest value appreciation this year may look like an underappreciated growth story. But it’s evident that PDD’s tripped up in a series of emerging fundamental headwinds that’ll be challenging to fix. The structural challenge facing PDD’s business model leaves little near-term respite for its topline. Meanwhile, the consistent pace of earnings expansion observed in FY24 shows limited durability, as operational costs increase in the face of challenged growth. And on top of the operational challenges, PDD also faces external tariff threats and share dilution risks.

Although PDD’s trading at a relative discount to peers with a similar growth profile, the company exhibits limited catalyst in sight that’d be capable of narrowing the gap. It’s also exposed to impending downside risks that could pressure valuations. With U.S. tech valuations starting to look more reasonably priced in the post-Q1 selloff, and signs of global investors recalibrating their China risk exposure, PDD seems vulnerable. Since fundamentals always take precedence in determining stock valuations, PDD’s underlying weakness represents a threat that shouldn’t be overlooked.
